concepts.title: Section Formula
concepts.conceptBody:
Section formula

Thus, the position vector of the midpoint of A B is $\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}+\vec{b})$.

(ii) External Division 

Let $A$ and $B$ be two points with position vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ respectively and let $C$ be a point dividing $\text{AB}$ externally in the ratio $\text{m}:\text{n}$, Then the position vector of $\mathbf{C}$ is given by

$\overrightarrow{\text{OC}}=\frac{\text{mb}-\text{n}\overrightarrow{\text{a}}}{\text{m}-\text{n}}$

Q) Find the position vector of the point which divide the joint of points $2\vec{a}-3\vec{b}$ and $3\vec{a}-2\vec{b}$ internally and externally in the ratio 2: 3.

Solution: 

Let $A$ and $B$ be the given points with position vectors $2\vec{a}-3\vec{b}$ and $3\vec{a}-2\vec{b}$ respectively. Let $P$ and $\text{Q}$ be the points dividing $\text{AB}$ in the ratio 2: 3 internally and externally respectively. Then,

Position vector of $\text{ }P=\frac{3(2\vec{a}-3\vec{b})+2(3\vec{a}-2\vec{b})}{3+2}$

$=\frac{12\vec{a}}{5}-\frac{13\vec{b}}{5}$

Position vector of $\text{ }Q=\frac{3(2\vec{a}-3\vec{b})-2(3\vec{a}-2\vec{b})}{3-2}=-5\vec{b}$

Q) Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be the position vectors of three distinct points A, B, C. If there exists scalar x, y, z (not all zero) such that$x\vec{a}+y\vec{b}+z\vec{c}=\vec{0}$ and $x+y+z=0$, then show that A, B and C lie on a line.

Solution: 

It is given that $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$ are not all zero. So, let $\text{z}$ be non-zero.

Then $x\vec{a}+y\vec{b}+z\vec{c}=\vec{0}$

$\Rightarrow $ $z\vec{c}=-(x\vec{a}+y\vec{b})$ $\Rightarrow $ $\vec{c}=-\frac{(x\vec{a}+y\vec{b})}{z}$

$\Rightarrow $ $\vec{c}=\frac{x\vec{a}+y\vec{b}}{x+y}$ $[\because x+y+z=0\therefore z=-(x+y)]$

This shows that the point $\text{C}$ divides the line joining the points $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ in the ratio $\text{y}:\text{x}$. hence, $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ lie on the same line.

concepts.title: Sine Rule
concepts.conceptBody:
Sine rule

In a triangle $ABC$ we have seen that

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{area}=\text{ a}\text{.c}\text{.sin } \text{ }\left( \text{B} \right)/2=\text{ b}\text{.c}\text{. } \text{ sin}\left( \text{A} \right)/2=\text{ a}\text{.b}\text{. } \text{ sin}\left( \text{C} \right)/2 \\ {} & \text{ }\Rightarrow \text{a}\text{.c}\text{. } \text{ sin}\left( \text{B} \right)=\text{ b}\text{.c}\text{. } \text{ sin}\left( \text{A} \right)=\text{ a}\text{.b}\text{.sin } \text{ }\left( \text{C} \right) \\ {} & \text{ dividing } \text{ through } \text{ out } \text{ by } \text{ a}\text{.b}\text{.c}\text{., } \text{ we } \text{ get } \\ \end{array}$

In any triangle $\text{ABC}$ we have

\(\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}\)

concepts.title: Section Formula for vectors
concepts.conceptBody:
Section Formula 

It is divided into two parts, for point $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ dividing the 2 points $\text{A}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ & $\text{B}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ internally or externally.

(i) When $\text{P}$ divides $\text{AB}$ internally

let the point $\text{P}$ divide the line joining the points $\text{A}\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}$ in the ratio $\text{m}:\text{n}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{AP}}{\text{PB}}=\frac{\text{m}}{\text{n}}$

Then the coordinates of point $\text{P}$

$\text{x}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{n}{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}+\text{n}}$

$\text{y}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{n}{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{\text{m}+\text{n}}$

Tip : To remember the formula, multiply $\text{m}$ with the coordinates of the point opposite to it, i.e. here with $B$, and similarly $n$ with $A$.

(ii) When $\text{P}$ divides $\text{AB}$ externally

Here $\text{P}$ divides $\text{AB}$ externally in the ratio

$\frac{\text{AP}}{\text{PB}}=\frac{\text{m}}{\text{n}}$

then the coordinates of $\text{P}$ are

$\text{x}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{x}}_{2}}-\text{n}{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}-\text{n}}$

$\text{y}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{y}}_{2}}-\text{ny}}{\text{m}-\text{n}}$

Note: Using the same formula we can say that the midpoint of $AB$ will be

$\left( \frac{{{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}}{2},\frac{{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{y}}_{2}}}{2} \right)$

Working rule 

(i) If you have to find the ratio in which a point divides the line segment, let the ratio be $\text{m}$ $:\text{n}$ (i.e. you take for granted that it is an internal division). But if you get a negative ratio then that means that the division is external.

(ii) Instead of taking the ratio to be $\text{m}:\text{n}$, we will take it to be $\lambda :1$. The advantage in the later case is that it has only one new variable against to 2 new variables $\text{m},\text{n}$ in previous case.

Here also, if $\lambda >0$, internal division

$\lambda <0$, external division $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

The line joining the points $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ is divided by the $\text{x}$-axis in the ratio $-\frac{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{{{\text{y}}_{2}}}$ and by $\text{y}$-axis in the ratio $-\frac{{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{{{\text{x}}_{2}}}$.

In square, rhombus, rectangle and parallelogram diagonals bisect to each other.

concepts.title: Principal Values
concepts.conceptBody:
Methods for finding principle values

Suppose we have to find the principal value of $\theta $ satisfying the equation $\text{sin}\theta =-\frac{1}{2}$

Since $\text{sin}\theta $ is negative, $\theta $ will be in 3 rd or 4 th quadrant. We can approach 3rd or 4th quadrant from two directions. If we take anticlockwise direction the numerical value of the angle will be greater than $\pi $. If we approach it in clockwise direction the angle will be numerically less than $\pi $. For principal value, we have to take numerically smallest angle.

So for principal value:

(i) If the angle is in 1st or 2nd quadrant we must select anticlockwise direction and if the angle is in 3rd or 4th quadrant, we must select clockwise direction.

(ii) Principal value is never numerically greater than $\pi $.

(iii) Principal value always lies in the first circle (i.e. in first rotation). On the above criteria, $\theta $ will be $-\frac{\pi }{6}$ or $-\frac{5\pi }{6}$. Among these two $-\frac{\pi }{6}$ has the least numerical value. Hence $-\frac{\pi }{6}$ is the principal value of $\theta $ satisfying the equation$\text{sin}\theta =-\frac{1}{2}$.

From the above discussion, the method for finding principal value can be summed up as follows:

(a) First draw a trigonometric circle and mark the quadrant, in which the angle may lie.

(b) Select anticlockwise direction for 1st and 2nd quadrants and select clockwise direction for 3rd and 4th quadrants.

(c) Find the angle in the first rotation.

(d) Select the numerically least angle among these two values. The angle thus found will be the principal value.

(e) Incase, two angles one with positive sign and the other with negative sign qualify for the numerically least angle, then it is the convention to select the angle with positive sign as principal value.

concepts.title: Vector and Scalar
concepts.conceptBody:
Scalars Vs. Vectors

Physical quantities are divided into 2 categories

(a) Scalar

(b) Vectors

Scalars: The quantities which have only magnitude and which are not related to any fixed direction in space.

ex. distance.

Vectors: The quantities which have both

(i) magnitude &

(ii) direction

ex. displacement.

Note: Scalars are just real numbers & hence are added, subtracted, multiplied etc. like numbers.

Representation & Notation of Vectors

The vectors are generally denoted as $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ etc. Geometrically vectors are nothing but a directed line segment.

So vector for the given line is $\overrightarrow{\text{PQ}}$, where $\text{P}$ is the initial point & $\text{Q}$ is the ending point or tip.

Magnitude of a vector:

The modulus / magnitude of a vector is the measure of its length and is denoted by $|\vec{a}|$ for a vector $\vec{a}$. It is also written as simply a.

concepts.title: Types of Vector
concepts.conceptBody:
Types of Vector

(i) Zero Vector: It is nothing but a vector with magnitude zero. Denoted by $\vec{0}$ and geometrically it just represents a point.

(ii) Unit Vector: It is a vector whose magnitude is unity. i.e.

$|\vec{a}|=1$

It is denoted by ã (i.e. a cap)

Important: If $\vec{a}$ is a vector whose magnitude is a, then the unit vector in direction of a is $\tilde{a}$ & is obtained by

$\tilde{a}=\frac{{\vec{a}}}{|a|}$

Note: Any two unit vectors $\tilde{a}\,\,\And \,\,\tilde{b}$ should not be taken to equal as you never know about their direction & for vector’s to be equal, both direction & magnitude should be equal.

(iii) Negative Vector: If a be a vector, then -a is a negative vector, whose magnitude is equal to that of $\bar{a}$ but has opposite direction.

$\xrightarrow{\overline{a}}\xleftarrow{-\overline{a}}$

$|a|\,=\,|-\bar{a}|$

(iv) Equal Vectors: Two vectors are equal if and only if, both their direction and magnitude is equal. Here $\overrightarrow{a}\,\,\And \,\,\overrightarrow{b}$ are parallel $|\overline{a}|\,=\,|\overline{b}|$

(v) Like & Unlike Vectors: Two vectors are said to be like if they have same direction (not necessarily same magnitude). & unlike, if they have opposite directions.

concepts.title: Addition of Vectors
concepts.conceptBody:
Algebra of vectors

(I) Addition of Vectors

Parallelogram Law of Addition: If two vectors lie along 2 adjacent sides of a parallelogram then the diagonal through the common vertex represent their sum

i.e. $\vec{\text{s}}=\vec{\text{a}}+\vec{\text{b}}$

Triangle Law of addition: If 2 vectors are represented as 2 sides of a triangle taken in order, then their sum is represented by third side taken in reverse order.

$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{\text{c}}=\vec{\text{a}}+\vec{\text{b}}$

These 2 laws can be extended to any polygon for example

here $\vec{\text{f}}=\vec{\text{a}}+\vec{\text{b}}+\vec{\text{c}}+\vec{\text{d}}+\vec{\text{e}}$

Properties of addition of vectors: 

1. Vector addition is commutative i.e. $\vec{a}+\vec{b}=\vec{b}+\vec{a}$

2. Vector addition is associative i.e. $\vec{\text{a}}+(\vec{\text{b}}+\vec{\text{c}})=(\vec{\text{a}}+\vec{\text{b}})+\vec{\text{c}}$

3. $|\vec{\text{a}}+\vec{\text{b}}|\le |\vec{\text{a}}|+|\vec{\text{b}}|$

and equality lies when both vectors have same direction,

i.e. $\begin{align} & \xrightarrow[{}]{{\vec{a}}}\xrightarrow[{}]{{\vec{b}}}  \xrightarrow[\vec{a}+\vec{b}]{{}} \end{align}$

concepts.title: Subtraction of Vectors
concepts.conceptBody:
Subtraction of Vectors

Property: 

||$\overline{\text{a}}|-|\overline{\text{b}}||\le |\overline{\text{a}}+\overline{\text{b}}|$

concepts.title: Multiplication of Vector by Scalar
concepts.conceptBody:
Multiplication of a vector by a scalar

If we multiply any vector with any scalar, then following cases can occur.

Taking $\overline{\text{a}}$ as vector quantity & $\text{k}$ as scalar

1. If $\text{k}>0$, then $\text{k}\overline{\text{a}}$ represents a vector whose direction is same as that of $\overline{\text{a}}$ but with a magnitude $\text{k}$ times a.

2. If $\text{k}<0$, then $\text{k}\overline{\text{a}}$ represents a vector opposite in direction having magnitude $\text{k}$ times that of $\overline{\text{a}}$.

Properties of multiplication 

1. $\text{k}(-\overline{\text{a}})=(-\text{k})\overline{\text{a}}=-(\text{k}\overline{\text{a}})$ 

2. $(-\text{k})(-\overline{\text{a}})=\text{k}\overline{\text{a}}$

3. $\text{k}(\text{m}\overline{\text{a}})=\text{km}\overline{\text{a}}$ 

4. $(p+q)\bar{a}=p\bar{a}+q\bar{a}$

Q) If the vector $\bar{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ represent two adjacent sides of a regular hexagon, express the other sides as vectors in terms of $\bar{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

Solution:

concepts.title: Linearly dependent & independent system
concepts.conceptBody:
Liner Combination of Vectors

If a vector $r$ can be represented as $r=x\bar{a}+y\bar{b}+z\bar{c}$ where $\bar{a},\bar{b}~\And~\bar{c}$ are vectors & $x,y~\And~ z$ are scalars, then $\text{r}$ is a linear combination of vectors $\overline{\text{a}},\overline{\text{b}}~\And~ \overline{\text{c}}$.

Linearly dependent & independent system: 

1. A System of vectors ${{a}_{1}}{{a}_{2}}{{a}_{3}}\ldots {{a}_{n}}$ is said to be linearly dependent if there exists a system of scalars ${{\lambda }_{1}}{{\lambda }_{2}}{{\lambda }_{3}}\ldots {{\lambda }_{n}}$ such that ${{\lambda }_{1}}{{\overline{\text{a}}}_{1}}+{{\lambda }_{2}}{{\overline{\text{a}}}_{2}}\ldots {{\lambda }_{\text{n}}}{{\overline{\text{a}}}_{\text{n}}}=0$ where all ${{\lambda }_{1}}\ldots {{\lambda }_{\text{n}}}$ are not zero at the same time

concepts.title: Collinearity
concepts.conceptBody:
Collinearity 

Collinear Vectors: Two vectors $\bar{a}\,\,\And \,\,\bar{b}$ are collinear if they are on same or parallel lines

i.e.

Thus, if $\overline{\text{a}},\overline{\text{b}}$ are 2 collinear vectors then $\overline{\text{a}}=\lambda \overline{\text{b}}$ or $\overline{\text{b}}=\lambda \overline{\text{a}}$ for some scalar $\lambda $.

Also, if 2 vectors $\overline{\text{a}}~\And~\overline{\text{b}}$ are non-collinear and $\text{x }~\And~\text{ y}$ are scalars such that

$\text{x}\overline{\text{a}}+\text{y}\overline{\text{b}}=0,\quad \text{ then }\xrightarrow{{}}\text{x}=\text{y}=0$

To Prove that 3 points are collinear, we form 2 vectors out of them & then prove these vectors to be collinear.

for example, if$\text{A},\text{B }~\And~\text{ C}$ are 3 points, we form 2 vectors $\text{AB }~\And~\text{ BC }~\And~$ now will prove then collinear.

If 2 points are non-collinear then if

$p\bar{a}+q\bar{b}=x\bar{a}+y\bar{b}\Rightarrow p=x~\And~q=y$

Note: 2 vectors are always co-planar.

Collinearity of 3 points

To prove 3 points with position vectors $\overline{\text{a}},\overline{\text{b}}~\And~ \overline{\text{c}}$ respectively:

$\overline{\text{a}},\overline{\text{b}}~\And~ \overline{\text{c}}$ are collinear if there exists scalars $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$ not all zero such that

concepts.title: Coplanarity
concepts.conceptBody:
Coplanarity

Coplanar Vectors : A set of vectors is said to be coplanar, if they lie in same plane. As already stated any two vectors will constitute a plane.

(I) Method to prove 3 vectors to be coplanar: 

Three vectors $\bar{a},\bar{b},\bar{c}$ will be coplanar if one of them can be expressed as linear combination of the remaining two vectors.

i.e. $\overline{\text{c}}=\lambda \overline{\text{b}}+\mu \overline{\text{c}}\quad \text{ (where }\lambda ~\And ~\mu \text{ are scalar) }$

(II) Method to prove 4 points to be coplanar: 

Method-1: To prove that 4 points A,B,C,D are coplanar, make 3 vectors$\overline{\text{AB}},\overline{\text{AC}}\text{ }\And\text{ }\overline{\text{AD}}\text{ }\And\text{ }$prove them to be coplanar by above method.

Method-2: Given that position vectors of 4 points are$\overline{\text{a}},\overline{\text{b}},\overline{\text{c}}~\And~\overline{\text{d}}$respectively, then these points are coplanar if

(i) $x\bar{a}+y\bar{b}+z\bar{c}+w\bar{d}=0,$ &

(ii) $\text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}+\text{w}=0$

where $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}~\And~\text{w}$ are not all zero at the same time.

Q) Derive that Any three non-coplanar vectors are linearly independent.

Solution: 

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be three non-coplanar vector and let $x\vec{a}+y\vec{b}+z\vec{c}=\vec{0}$ for some scalar x, y, z. In order to prove that $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are linearly independent we must show that $x=y=z=0$.

If possible, let $x\ne 0$. Then

$x\vec{a}+y\vec{b}+z\vec{c}=\vec{0}$$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}=\left( -\frac{y}{x} \right)\vec{b}+-\left( \frac{z}{x} \right)\vec{c}[\because x\ne 0]$

$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}$ lies in the plane of $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are coplanar vectors.

This is a contradiction. So $\text{x}=0$. Similarly, we can show that $\text{y}=\text{z}=0$.

Hence, $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are linearly independent vectors.

Note: Since $\tilde{i},\tilde{j},\tilde{k}$ are non-coplanar vectors, therefore $\tilde{i},\tilde{j},\tilde{k}$ are linearly independent unit vectors.
